title: Callback: NeRFs Meet Diffusion
concept: Callback to Unit 174: many 3D generation methods represent the object as a Neural Radiance Field, optimized using guidance from a 2D diffusion model.

exercises.prompt: Quick refresher: what is a NeRF (Neural Radiance Field)?

options.text: A neural network that learns to represent a 3D scene, so you can render it from any new angle
A type of camera used to take 3D photos
A way to compress 2D images into smaller files

exercises.explain: Right! A NeRF is a neural network trained to capture a 3D scene's shape and color, letting you generate views from angles never actually photographed.

cards.title: What if you could sculpt with imagination?
cards.body: Remember NeRFs from Unit 174 — those neural networks that build 3D scenes from photos? Now imagine skipping the photos entirely. What if you just typed 'a small red dragon statue' and a 3D model appeared? That's exactly what happens when NeRFs team up with diffusion models!

cards.title: NeRF + Diffusion, Together
cards.body: Many text-to-3D AI systems build a NeRF to represent the 3D object, then use a 2D diffusion model as a 'judge.' The diffusion model checks if random 2D views of the NeRF look like a good image matching the text prompt. The NeRF slowly updates itself until every angle looks correct — no 3D training data needed!

cards.title: The Blindfolded Sculptor
cards.body: Picture a sculptor (the NeRF) working on a clay statue. They can't see the whole thing at once, so they keep asking a friend (the diffusion model) to look at it from one side at a time and say 'more like a dragon here' or 'that looks off.' Bit by bit, using feedback from every angle, the sculptor shapes a statue that looks right from all sides — even though they never got to see a finished 3D dragon to copy.

cards.title: DreamFusion Style Pipeline
cards.body:
1) Start with a random, blurry NeRF blob.
2) Render a random 2D view of it.
3) Ask a 2D diffusion model: 'Does this look like a red dragon statue?'
4) Use the diffusion model's feedback to nudge the NeRF's shape and color.
5) Repeat from many angles thousands of times until the NeRF becomes a clean, recognizable 3D dragon.

cards.title: Myth: You need 3D scans to train these models
cards.body:
Myth: To create 3D objects with AI, you must feed it tons of 3D scanned data.
Reality: These NeRF + diffusion systems often need ZERO 3D training examples! They only use a 2D diffusion model (trained on flat images) as guidance, checking every 2D 'view' of the evolving 3D shape.

cards.title: Two Ways to Get a 3D Model
cards.body: Compare the classic NeRF approach with the new diffusion-guided approach.

left.text: Needs many real photos of ONE specific object from different angles to rebuild it in 3D.

right.text: Needs just a TEXT PROMPT — a 2D diffusion model guides the NeRF from imagination, no real photos required.

exercises.prompt: In a NeRF + diffusion 3D generation pipeline, what role does the 2D diffusion model play?

options.text: It stores the final 3D mesh file
It judges random 2D renders of the NeRF and gives feedback to guide its shape
It takes real photographs of the object
It converts the NeRF into a video game engine

exercises.explain: The 2D diffusion model acts like a critic, checking if rendered views match the text prompt, and that feedback nudges the NeRF to improve.

exercises.prompt: True or False: These text-to-3D systems require thousands of real 3D scanned objects to learn from.
exercises.explain: False! That's the surprising part — they only need a 2D diffusion model trained on flat images. No 3D training data is required.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the idea:
exercises.sentence: In text-to-3D generation, a 2D ___ model provides guidance to help shape the NeRF into the object described by the prompt.
exercises.explain: The 2D diffusion model provides the 'does this look right?' feedback that guides the NeRF's shape over many training steps.

exercises.prompt: A blindfolded sculptor keeps asking a friend to check the statue from different angles and says 'shape it more like this' each time. Which AI concept does the FRIEND represent?
exercises.explain: The friend giving feedback from each angle is like the 2D diffusion model, judging renders and guiding the sculptor (NeRF) toward the right shape.

exercises.prompt: Boss Question: Why can NeRF + diffusion pipelines create a 3D dragon statue from just a text prompt, without ever seeing a real 3D dragon?

options.text: Because the NeRF secretly downloads 3D models from the internet
Because a 2D diffusion model checks random rendered views of the NeRF against the prompt, and this feedback slowly shapes the NeRF into a consistent 3D object
Because diffusion models can directly output 3D meshes without any NeRF involved
Because the NeRF randomly guesses shapes until one looks correct by luck

exercises.explain: Exactly! The NeRF starts as a rough blob. By repeatedly rendering 2D views and getting 'does this match the prompt?' feedback from a 2D diffusion model, the NeRF gradually updates until it looks correct from every angle — turning 2D image knowledge into a full 3D object.
